# Azure-Integrated Hybrid Execution Plan for Capability‑Native Agent Governance

## Milestone 1 – Foundation (Azure Integration & Core Architecture)

**Duration:** 2 Sprints (Weeks 1–4)
**Objective:** Establish the core capability governance framework in an **Azure-centric environment**, ensuring it is built on **Azure-native identity, key management, and orchestration services** while architecting for future multi-cloud and decentralized identity support.

### Sprint 1 (Weeks 1–2) – Azure Identity & Environment Setup

**Objectives:**

*   Finalize the capability data model and token format, including cryptographic design and integration with key management.
*   Set up the development and test environment (code repositories, CI pipelines, dev cluster).
*   Align all teams on interfaces between components (APIs, message formats, logging schema).
*   Implement minimal scaffolding for each component (Issuer, Gateway, etc.) to allow early integration testing.

***

**Team CP (Control Plane) – Tasks:**

*   **Azure AD (Entra ID) Integration:** Implement initial user authentication and authorization flows using **Microsoft Entra ID**. Developers will use **OAuth 2.0 / OIDC** to authenticate a human user (or service principal) who initiates agent creation. The Zero-Trust Agents reference architecture from the Microsoft Foundry Blog demonstrates this pattern: each AI agent receives an **identity and issued access token for every action**, ensuring *"no implicit trust"* between entities and requiring that *"every interaction is authenticated and authorized, following the principle of 'never trust, always verify'"*. [\[techcommun...rosoft.com\]](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/azure-ai-foundry-blog/zero-trust-agents-adding-identity-and-access-to-multi-agent-workflows/4427790)
    *   *Deliverable:* A CLI or web interface for launching an agent after a successful Azure AD login/consent. This interface will retrieve an **OIDC token** for the user's identity (e.g., using MSAL library). The token's claims (user ID, roles/scopes) will be passed to the **Capability Issuer** service.
    *   *Azure Integration:* Register an **Azure AD application** for the agent system and configure user consent for necessary permissions. Use **Azure Managed Identity** for service-to-service auth (e.g., the Capability Issuer authenticating to Key Vault or Graph API).
    *   *Hybrid/Portable Design:* Architect the identity interface to be **pluggable** by following OpenID Connect standards and abstracting the identity provider. The Zero-Trust reference implementation achieves vendor neutrality by using WSO2 Asgardeo (an open-source IAM system) as its OAuth2 authorization server alongside Azure OpenAI, demonstrating that the same pattern works with non-Microsoft identity providers. Implement an **Identity Provider interface** that encapsulates token validation and user attribute retrieval, with one concrete implementation for Azure AD in the pilot. Document how additional providers (e.g., Okta, AWS Cognito, Google Cloud IAM) could implement this interface. [\[techcommun...rosoft.com\]](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/azure-ai-foundry-blog/zero-trust-agents-adding-identity-and-access-to-multi-agent-workflows/4427790)

*   **Capability Issuer & Token Format:** Build the initial **Capability Issuer** microservice.
    *   *Token Format & Signing:* Use a **JWT-based format** compliant with the W3C Verifiable Credentials Data Model, or a simplified JWT with custom claims for capabilities (to be upgraded to full VC later). The token should include standard claims (`iss`, `sub`, `aud`, `exp`) to ensure global verifiability. The **issuer (`iss`) claim** should be set to the enterprise's domain URL or DID (using `did:web` anchored in DNS). In decentralized identity systems, *"both the issuer and verifier have a DID, and a DID document"* containing public key information and linked domain references, allowing cross-domain cryptographic verification of credentials. [\[learn.microsoft.com\]](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/verified-id/introduction-to-verifiable-credentials-architecture)
    *   *Azure Key Vault Integration:* Generate or import a cryptographic key (e.g., RSA or ECC) in **Azure Key Vault** and assign the Capability Issuer's Managed Identity access to perform sign operations. As detailed in the auditable security layer architecture for agentic AI, Key Vault's sign operation creates a signature from a digest — *"Key Vault does not hash your content for you. Hash locally, then sign the digest"*. This leverages Azure's HSM-backed key management. The approach is validated by the reference code pattern: [\[azurefeeds.com\]](https://azurefeeds.com/2026/04/22/building-an-auditable-security-layer-for-agentic-ai/)
        ```typescript
        const sha256 = (x: unknown): string =>
          crypto.createHash("sha256").update(JSON.stringify(x)).digest("hex");
        ```
        Hashing is performed locally before calling Key Vault for the actual signature operation【5†L291-L294】.
    *   *Issuance Logic:* Implement the `/issue` endpoint. On receiving an authenticated user context, the Issuer will: (a) look up the user's Azure AD roles/claims, and (b) load a corresponding **Agent Capability Manifest template**. For the pilot, define a simple static mapping — e.g., if the user has AD role "SalesManager", the agent gets read/write capability for customer data; if "Viewer", read-only. Sign this token via Key Vault.
    *   *Distributed Identity Prep:* Document how the JWT could be accompanied by a **DID for the user and agent** as subject identifiers, and the signing key associated with the enterprise's DID Document. Research demonstrates that combining AI agents with W3C DIDs and W3C VCs enables cross-domain trust establishment — agents can *"prove ownership of their self-controlled DIDs for authentication purposes and establish various cross-domain trust relationships through the spontaneous exchange of their self-hosted DID-bound VCs"*【1†L40】. This paper, accepted at the 18th International Conference on Agents and Artificial Intelligence 2026, confirms technical feasibility but also identifies limitations when an agent's LLM is in sole charge of controlling security procedures — reinforcing the need for external mechanical enforcement【1†L40-L44】.

*   **Exit Criteria (Sprint 1):**
    *   A basic end-to-end issue flow is functional: after user login, the Issuer returns a signed JWT.
    *   Token contains correct claims (subject = agent identity, issuer = enterprise authority, permitted actions, \~15-minute expiration).
    *   Token is signed by the configured Azure Key Vault key (verifiable using the corresponding public key).
    *   Token is audience-restricted (contains an `aud` claim for the Tool Gateway).
    *   Cross-team dependency satisfied: **Team OBS** has logging enabled so every issuance is recorded.

***

**Team DP (Data Plane) – Tasks:**

*   **Agent Environment & Sandbox:** Establish the runtime using **Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)** or **Azure Container Instances**.
    *   Apply Kubernetes NetworkPolicies to restrict egress (allow only traffic to the Tool Gateway service and required Microsoft endpoints). The core design principle: *"the model is not your boundary"* — treat the model like an untrusted proposer and the runtime like the verifier, where each gate is external to the model and survives manipulation【5†L40-L42】.
    *   Run agent containers with a read-only root filesystem and least-privilege Linux capabilities.
    *   Mount only necessary volumes (ephemeral scratch) and ensure no sensitive host paths are accessible.
    *   *Exit Criteria:* A sample agent container can call a test endpoint through the Gateway but fails to reach disallowed endpoints. Running `curl` to an unauthorized URL from within the container should be blocked.

*   **Tool/Action Gateway (v1) – Azure APIM Integration:** Implement the Tool Gateway using **Azure API Management (APIM)** as the enforcement point. APIM is the canonical tool gateway for agent systems — *"the design goal is simple: the model can request a tool call. The gateway decides if it is permitted"*【5†L271-L273】.
    *   Configure APIM with a **`validate-jwt` policy** — described as *"the canonical enforcement mechanism for validating JWTs at the gateway"*【5†L269】. This policy accepts tokens signed by the Key Vault key and rejects requests with missing or invalid tokens (returning 401/403).
    *   Set up two example operations: one requiring a "read" scope and another requiring a "write" scope. Verify scope-based enforcement: *"you can enforce 'read-only mode' by issuing tokens that simply do not carry write scopes. The model can try to call a write tool. It still gets denied by policy"*【5†L281】.
    *   *Exit Criteria:* Agent with valid token and correct scope → action allowed. Agent with missing, invalid, or insufficient token → action denied with proper error code. APIM logs confirm authorization decisions.

***

**Team OBS (Observability & Compliance) – Tasks:**

*   **Audit Log Schema:** Define the initial **Capability Audit Log schema** with fields for timestamp, agent/session ID, action, resource, capability ID (or token hash), decision (allow/deny), and metadata.
*   **Logging Pipeline Setup:** Configure **Azure Monitor / Log Analytics** to collect logs from the Capability Issuer, APIM, and agent runtime. Enable APIM diagnostic logging.
*   **Monitoring Plan:** Identify critical conditions: repeated `DeniedAction` events (possible prompt injection), high-frequency tool calls (possible runaway loop). The detection layer should match the gates built: *"repeated Prompt Shields detections from the same identity or session," "tool-call spikes after a suspicious document signal," and "APIM denials for write endpoints from sessions in read-only mode"*【5†L357-L359】.

***

**Team DX (Developer Experience & Testing) – Tasks:**

*   **Agent Manifest Workflow:** Define how engineers specify an agent's capabilities via declarative YAML manifests. Develop example manifests for pilot agents.
*   **Test Plan:** Write unit, integration, and security test scenarios. Key test cases: forging a token should fail signature check; agent without required token should be denied; expired token should be rejected.

***

### Sprint 2 (Weeks 3–4) – Core System Hardening & Policy Integration

**Objectives:**

*   Implement genuine capability enforcement for critical action types (moving from stubs to real allow/deny decisions).
*   Connect the Capability Issuer to enterprise identity for policy-driven issuance.
*   Introduce evidence-grade audit logging with cryptographic integrity.

***

**Team CP – Tasks:**

*   **Policy-Driven Issuance:** Connect the Capability Issuer to live Azure AD via Microsoft Graph API. Fetch user roles and group memberships to determine which capabilities to issue. Test that two users with different Azure AD roles produce different capability sets for their agents.
*   **Token Signing & Verification:** Implement actual **token signing** using Azure Key Vault and update the Tool Gateway to **verify token signatures** using the public key. Ensure tokens include a short expiration (15 minutes).
*   **Capability Data Model:** Code the internal representation of capabilities. Include support for basic constraints: resource, action list, TTL. Advanced constraints (data redaction, rate limits) deferred to Sprint 3.

***

**Team DP – Tasks:**

*   **Enforcement Engine v1:** Upgrade the Tool Gateway beyond stub mode. Parse incoming requests to extract the capability token. Implement the validation chain:
    1.  **Signature verification** (using public key from Team CP). Invalid → reject with `403 Forbidden`.
    2.  **Payload validation:** Parse token claims, extract allowed actions/resources, check against the requested action.
    3.  **Expiration check:** Past `exp` → reject with `401 Unauthorized`.
    4.  Only if all checks pass → forward the request.
*   **Sandbox Refinement (Networking):** Ensure *all* external communications funnel through the Gateway. Adjust container network namespace to redirect outgoing HTTP(S) traffic to the Gateway's proxy port.

***

**Team OBS – Tasks:**

*   **Evidence Generation (Cryptographic Audit):** Move beyond standard logging to **signed evidence chains**. The principle: *"Logs help you debug. Evidence helps you prove"*【5†L285】. For each privileged action, hash the session envelope and tool intent, then sign the digest using Azure Key Vault Keys. The signed evidence schema should include:
    *   `sessionId`, `userId`, `promptHash`, `documentsHash`, `tool`, `argsHash`, `nonce`, `ts`, `policyVersion`【5†L296-L306】
    *   This creates tamper-evident audit records verifiable by any party with the enterprise's public key.
*   **Basic Kill-Switch:** Implement a rudimentary kill-switch for development use. A global flag checked by the Tool Gateway: if `KILL_ALL_AGENTS=true`, reject all requests. The design constraint: place kill switches in a control plane **outside the agent's runtime** for reliability and auditability.
*   **Monitoring Dashboards:** Stand up an initial dashboard displaying: actions executed, denied actions, active agent sessions. Define an alert for critical conditions (>5 denied actions/minute triggers alert).

***

**Team DX – Tasks:**

*   **Agent SDK Integration:** Modify the pilot agent's code to retrieve and attach capability tokens. At startup, the agent calls the Issuer's `/issue` endpoint. Every tool invocation includes the token via middleware or proxy configuration.
*   **Unit Tests:** Valid tokens accepted; invalid tokens rejected; expired token rejected; agent obtains new token and proceeds.

***

**Exit Criteria (Milestone 1):**

*   A working **Azure-integrated agent issuance and enforcement pipeline**: user logs in via Azure AD, launches an agent, and the agent calls a test service through APIM by presenting a valid token. Unauthorized actions are blocked.
*   No direct network egress from the agent except through the gateway.
*   All components running in Azure using Azure services (AD, Key Vault, APIM, AKS, Log Analytics).
*   Design notes explicitly account for future **hybrid extensions** (trusting non-Azure identity sources, different cloud infra for agents).
*   Gateway adds ≤1ms average latency per tool call.

***

## Milestone 2 – Extended Enforcement & Distributed Identity Foundations

**Goal:** Expand the system to support full coverage of agent actions and lay the groundwork for **cross-domain identity and distributed trust**. Implement robust **capability enforcement for all planned tool types** and introduce **decentralized identity components** for verification outside the Azure/enterprise boundary.

### Sprint 3 (Weeks 5–6) – Full Policy Coverage & DID Integration

***

**Team CP – Tasks:**

*   **Additional Capability Types:** Based on pilot agent functionality, define capabilities for all tool categories. Examples: `file_access` capabilities with allowed path/directory and actions (`read`/`write`); `api_invoke` capabilities with resource identifiers like `api://service-name/endpoint`. Update the Capability Schema.
*   **Delegation / Attenuation Mechanism:** Implement capability attenuation via a new Issuer endpoint (e.g., `/attenuate?parent_token=X&constraints=Y`). Enforce that child capability scope is a subset of parent scope (no new privileges). Support simplified subset operations (copy one token with reduced TTL) as the invariant holds.
*   **Establish Enterprise DID:** Register a Decentralized Identifier for the enterprise's issuer. For Azure, use **Microsoft Entra Verified ID**, which is designed to allow *"centralized and decentralized identity architectures to be combined to provide a solution that reduces risk and offers significant operational benefits"*【2†L73-L74】. Practical options:
    *   **`did:web`**: Anchored in DNS, where the enterprise hosts a DID Document at a well-known URL. In decentralized identity systems, a DID Document *"is accessible using a verifiable data registry and contains information related to a specific decentralized identifier, such as the associated repository and public key information"*【2†L63】. A trust system can be either a distributed ledger or *"something centralized, such as DID Web"*【2†L68】.
    *   **`did:ion`**: A public, permissionless DID network on Bitcoin (Microsoft is a contributor). Provides globally verifiable DIDs without central authority, at the cost of higher latency for updates.
    *   Generate a DID Document containing the enterprise's public keys (corresponding to the Key Vault key pair) and host it for verifier resolution.
*   **Agent DID Strategy:** Determine how each agent will be identified in a decentralized way. The Zero-Trust Agents reference implementation anticipates Microsoft's **Entra Agent ID** initiative, which *"similarly assigns unique Entra identities to AI agents for robust authentication and governance"*【9†L43】. Our design aligns with this by giving each agent a unique, verifiable identity.

***

**Team DP – Tasks:**

*   **Full Spectrum Tool Enforcement:** Extend the Tool Gateway to cover:
    *   **File system operations:** Use Azure **SAS tokens** for Azure Storage as part of capability issuance. The agent presents the SAS to Azure Storage API; access beyond the token's scope is denied.
    *   **Database queries:** Use token-based auth for Azure SQL DB. Generate short-lived DB access tokens as part of the agent's capability. The agent must use that token to connect — with only the permissions granted.
    *   **External HTTP requests:** Force all egress traffic through the gateway or an Azure Firewall. At minimum, monitor and log any external calls and whitelist domains.
*   **Sandbox Hardening:**
    *   Process runs as non-privileged OS user with AppArmor/SELinux profiles preventing ptrace, mount, and other dangerous syscalls.
    *   CPU and memory limits on agent containers via cgroups.
    *   Environment scrubbing: no sensitive info in agent environment variables by default.
*   **Delegation Enforcement:** Recognize and handle attenuated tokens. Track session/agent identity to distinguish parent vs. child agents and ensure children cannot bypass restrictions.

***

**Team OBS – Tasks:**

*   **Audit Log Schema Finalization:** Add a `ParentCapabilityID` field for delegation events linking child capabilities to their parent.
*   **Microsoft Sentinel Integration:** Set up **Microsoft Sentinel** to turn telemetry into incidents with scheduled analytics rules【5†L353】. Create analytic rules for:
    *   Repeated denied tool requests by the same agent.
    *   Tool-call spikes after a suspicious document signal.
    *   APIM denials for write endpoints from sessions in read-only mode.
    *   High-sensitivity label retrieval by identities that should never touch that tier【5†L357-L360】.
*   **Posture Management Integration:** Microsoft Defender for Cloud's Defender CSPM plan includes *"AI security posture management for generative AI apps and AI agents (Preview), including discovery/inventory of AI agents deployed with Azure AI Foundry"*【5†L351】. Enable this for automated discovery of agent deployments.
*   **Kill Switch v2 (Session-Scoped):** Extend from global kill to session-specific kill. Provide an admin API (authenticated, internal-only) to add a session to the kill list. Once killed, the Gateway rejects further tool calls from that session.

***

**Team DX – Tasks:**

*   **Developer CLI for Capabilities:** Provide a CLI tool for capability management — e.g., `agentcap init --agent SalesEmailAgent` generates a template YAML manifest.
*   **UAT Plan:** Work with intended pilot users to define UAT scenarios: agent sends email within allowed domain → succeeds; agent attempts email outside domain → blocked and logged; agent tries unapproved file write → blocked; agent with expired token fetches new token and succeeds.

***

**Exit Criteria (Sprint 3):**

*   All high-risk agent actions are mediated by the capability layer. Agent container with no capabilities issued **cannot** perform any external action.
*   Child agents cannot escalate privileges beyond explicit parent grants.
*   Enterprise DID is operational: other team members can resolve the DID and obtain the public key needed to verify credentials.
*   Static code analysis shows no critical vulnerabilities. Secrets scanning confirms no sensitive data in repos or logs.
*   10 concurrent agents at \~5 tool calls/min shows Gateway latency <5ms overhead per call at p95.

***

### Sprint 4 (Weeks 7–8) – Cross-Organization Trust & Pilot Preparation

***

**Team CP – Tasks:**

*   **Azure AD Full Integration:** Hook the Capability Issuer to live enterprise IAM. Use **Microsoft Graph API** to fetch user roles or group memberships. Honor Conditional Access policies and Privileged Identity Management (PIM) activations for time-bound elevated permissions.
*   **Finalize Renewal & Revocation:** Complete the `/renew` endpoint for seamless token refresh. Implement **explicit revocation**: when an operator revokes a capability, move the corresponding token ID to a revocation list. Ensure the Gateway syncs with this list within seconds.
*   **Verifiable Credential Issuance:** Upgrade capability tokens to **W3C Verifiable Credential format** using Microsoft Entra Verified ID or an equivalent library. In the VC model, a credential is *"a tamper-evident credential that has authorship that can be cryptographically verified"* and can be used to build verifiable presentations that are also cryptographically verified【2†L59】. The VC binds the agent's DID (subject) to specific capabilities (claims) with the enterprise's DID as issuer.

***

**Team DP – Tasks:**

*   **Cross-Organization Simulation:** Set up a controlled environment to test multi-organization trust:
    *   **Simulate a Partner Organization:** Instantiate a minimal "partner" service in a separate AKS namespace or Azure App Service. Configure it with your enterprise's DID public key as a trusted issuer. Test that an agent's VC is accepted.
    *   **Incoming Partner Agent:** Create a dummy VC signed by a "partner issuer" DID. Test that your Tool Gateway validates it by resolving the partner's DID Document. The Microsoft Entra Verified ID architecture demonstrates this pattern: *"Woodgrove is a trusted VC issuer in Proseware's validation solution. This trust in Woodgrove's issuance process allows Proseware to electronically accept the VC as proof"*【2†L57】.
*   **Performance & Scalability Testing:** Simulate 50 concurrent agents making rapid tool requests. Measure overhead of capability checks and cryptographic operations. Confirm 95th percentile latency within acceptable limits.
*   **Production Security Hardening:** Remove development backdoors. Use minimal OS base container images (alpine or distroless). Confirm no root access. Test container escape resilience.

***

**Team OBS – Tasks:**

*   **End-to-End Monitoring & DR:** Confirm all components have proper logging and error monitoring.
*   **Runbook and Incident Response:** Develop a runbook covering: how to recognize agent misbehavior (specific alert types), how to use the kill switch (step-by-step), how to verify the kill took effect (watch Gateway logs for 403 errors), and communication/escalation steps.
*   **Live Drill:** Intentionally trigger a scenario (e.g., a runaway agent in a test setting) and have the operator use the documented procedure.
*   **Compliance Review Prep:** Confirm sensitive data (PII, secrets) is not stored in logs or tokens. Verify pilot logs and controls align with applicable requirements.

***

**Team DX – Tasks:**

*   **Pilot Playbook:** Step-by-step guide for the operational team covering deployment, monitoring, error interpretation, and support contacts.
*   **User Testing in Staging:** Run UAT in near-production staging. Verify agents complete tasks, policy violations fail visibly, and logs capture events correctly.

***

**Exit Criteria (Milestone 2):**

*   All high-risk agent actions mediated by the capability layer — nothing bypasses without detection.
*   Credentials conform to open standards and can be verified using only the DID Document and standard JWT/VC libraries, without proprietary code.
*   The solution combines centralized and decentralized identity: Azure AD handles user authentication and initial approvals, agents operate using decentralized credentials externally. *"By combining centralized and decentralized identity architectures, the responsibility and effort associated with identity and proof of identity is distributed and risk is lowered"*【8†L1】.
*   Audit logs are cryptographically strengthened and capture complete trails. Evidence signatures are verifiable using the enterprise's public key【5†L283-L291】.
*   Sentinel alerts are tuned and the team has practiced incident response.

***

## Milestone 3 – Pilot Deployment & Cross-Cloud Demonstration

**Goal:** Deploy the system in a controlled production pilot with real (or realistic) data, including a demonstration of cross-cloud or cross-organization functionality. Emphasis on end-to-end security verification, performance, and preparation for scaling.

### Sprint 5 (Weeks 9–10) – Production Pilot Launch on Azure

***

**All Teams – Collaborative Tasks:**

*   **Production Deployment:** Deploy all components to the production environment:
    *   Capability Issuer as a highly-available service (two or more instances, behind load balancer, with auto-scale).
    *   Tool Gateway close to agents (sidecar in AKS pod, DaemonSet, or dedicated internal service).
    *   Confirm all credentials correctly set up (Key Vault access for signing key, Graph API permissions, Gateway's public key reference).
    *   Use **Azure Resource Manager templates or Bicep** for reproducible provisioning.

*   **Cloud-Portability Documentation:** Document cloud-specific pieces and equivalents elsewhere:
    *   APIM → AWS API Gateway + Lambda Authorizers
    *   AKS → EKS or GKE
    *   Azure Key Vault → AWS KMS or HashiCorp Vault
    *   Azure Monitor/Sentinel → CloudWatch/GuardDuty or Chronicle
    *   The core logic remains the same across clouds because the system relies on standard protocols (OIDC, OAuth, VC/JWT, DID).

*   **Go/No-Go Meeting:** Final review with all stakeholders. Present Milestone 2 results and compliance review. Proceed only with unanimous agreement.

*   **Pilot Launch:** Activate for real users. Coordinate with dependency teams.

*   **Hypercare:** Engineers from each team on standby. Monitor logs and dashboards in real time. Rapid issue response for any pilot issues.

*   **Collect Metrics & Feedback:** Actions performed vs. blocked. False positives vs. true positives. Token renewal frequency. Performance measurements. Alert trigger analysis.

***

**Azure Monitoring & Management:**

*   **Azure Monitor & Application Insights** for real-time performance monitoring.
*   **Microsoft Sentinel fine-tuning** with real pilot data. Adjust alert thresholds based on baseline behavior.
*   **Microsoft Purview integration** (if dealing with sensitive data): Purview sensitivity labels enable governing what the agent can see — *"Label the corpus. Filter retrieval by label and identity policy. Log label distribution per completion. Alert when a low-privilege identity retrieves high-sensitivity labels"*【5†L340-L343】.

***

**Exit Criteria (Sprint 5):**

*   Pilot deployed and running in production handling real agent actions.
*   At least one real user session completed successfully.
*   Gateway average latency overhead \~0.5–1ms, p99 < 5ms.
*   Issuer adds <1 second to agent launch for token retrieval.
*   No security breaches or serious incidents in initial period.
*   Documentation, runbooks, FAQs, and support processes finalized.

***

### Sprint 6 (Weeks 11–12) – Pilot Stabilization & Handoff

***

**All Teams – Tasks:**

*   **Bug Fixes and Tuning:** Address issues from Sprint 5 monitoring. Prioritize fixes reducing risk. All changes tested in staging before production.
*   **Finalize Alert Thresholds:** Adjust with real data. If 1–2 legitimate denials per day are normal, raise the "deny spike" threshold accordingly.
*   **Cross-Cloud Extension (Optional):** If resources and time permit, demonstrate portability by deploying a minimal version in another environment (e.g., AWS with Cognito, KMS, API Gateway). Issue a similar capability for an agent and verify cross-cloud acceptance by trusting the other environment's public key/DID.
*   **Documentation and Handoff:** Update Capability Manifest Guide with pilot-discovered patterns. Formalize ownership. Prepare next-steps backlog for full rollout.
*   **Next Steps Planning:** Develop backlog items for broad adoption: self-service UI for capability requests, dynamic policy engines, cross-cloud support, industry standards contributions.

***

**Exit Criteria (Milestone 3):**

*   Stable pilot operations with no major issues over ≥2 weeks of real use.
*   All pilot objectives met: no security incidents, successful prevention of policy violations, acceptable performance and user satisfaction.
*   Team handoff completed with formalized on-call rotations and ownership.
*   Go/No-Go review held with final pilot report prepared for leadership.

***

## End-to-End Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the full system architecture, including the Azure-based enterprise domain, a partner/external domain, and the decentralized trust infrastructure connecting them. Trust boundaries are represented by subgraph separation.

![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

**Trust Boundaries:**

*   **Enterprise Domain (left):** Issues capability credentials to agents via Entra ID-authenticated flows and enforces them via an Azure APIM gateway. All internal tool calls require valid capability tokens.
*   **Partner Domain (right):** Represents a partner organization or alternate cloud with its own identity issuer and agents.
*   **Decentralized Trust Infrastructure (center):** The public layer (DID registries, trust anchors, blockchain or DNS-based resolution) enabling cross-verification of DIDs and credentials without direct integration between identity providers.

**Cross-Domain Flows:**

1.  An internal agent calls an external service with its enterprise-issued VC. The external service resolves the enterprise's DID via the public trust infrastructure, verifies the credential signature, and grants access — no live call to Azure AD required.
2.  A partner agent calls an internal API with its partner-issued VC. The enterprise gateway resolves the partner's issuer DID, verifies the VC, and grants access if the partner is a trusted issuer for that API.

This architecture ensures that *"using a decentralized architecture with verifiable credentials can provide value by augmenting key scenarios such as... accessing resources outside the trust boundary, such as accessing partners' resources, with a portable credential issued by the organization"*【2†L33-L37】.

***

## Architecture Design Rationale

**Control Plane vs Data Plane Separation:**

| **Layer**                | **Responsibility**                                                                | **Azure Services**                                      | **Cloud-Agnostic Alternative**                                                             |
| ------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Control Plane**        | Capability issuance, policy decisions, DID management, key management, revocation | Entra ID, Key Vault, Entra Verified ID, Microsoft Graph | Any OIDC IdP, HashiCorp Vault, open-source DID tooling (Veramo, Hyperledger Aries)         |
| **Data Plane**           | Tool call interception, token validation, scope enforcement, action logging       | Azure APIM (`validate-jwt` policy), AKS NetworkPolicies | Any API gateway with JWT validation, Kubernetes on any cloud, NGINX/Envoy with custom auth |
| **Observability**        | Audit logging, evidence signing, SIEM integration, alerting                       | Azure Monitor, Log Analytics, Sentinel, Defender CSPM   | ELK stack, Splunk, any SIEM, open-source signing tools                                     |
| **Trust Infrastructure** | DID resolution, VC verification, revocation status checking                       | Entra Verified ID, did:web via Azure DNS                | ION network, did:key, any W3C DID resolver                                                 |

**Why APIM as Tool Gateway:** Azure API Management provides the `validate-jwt` policy as a built-in, high-performance enforcement mechanism. The capability token approach enables clean scope-based enforcement: issue tokens without write scopes to enforce read-only mode, and the gateway denies write attempts by policy regardless of what the model proposes【5†L275-L281】. This is a stateless, deterministic check that does not depend on prompt-level instructions or LLM behavior.

**Why Evidence Chains over Standard Logs:** Standard logs record events. Evidence chains **prove** events. By hashing session context (session ID, user ID, prompt hash, tool, arguments hash, nonce, timestamp, policy version) and signing the digest with Azure Key Vault, each privileged action produces a tamper-evident artifact that any party can independently verify【5†L296-L328】. This distinction is critical for compliance and forensics.

**Why DID/VC over Static API Keys:** Research on AI agents with DIDs and VCs demonstrates that equipping agents with *"long-lived digital identities"* combined with *"tamper-proof and flexible identity-bound attestations"* enables *"autonomous and interoperable trust establishment"* once agents operate *"beyond isolated environments and engage in dialogues across individual or organizational boundaries"*【1†L40】. Static API keys cannot support delegation chains, cross-org trust, or fine-grained capability scoping.

**Enterprise Hybrid Identity Model:** The recommended approach follows the pattern validated by Microsoft's Entra Verified ID architecture: *"decentralized architectures can be used to enhance existing solutions and provide new capabilities"* while centralized approaches *"still work well in many cases, such as when applications, services, and devices rely on the trust mechanisms used within a domain or trust boundary"*【2†L29-L33】. The ChainScore analysis argues that enterprise DID adoption requires a hybrid model because *"pure on-chain identity fails"* since enterprises will not discard existing *"Active Directory and SAML"* investments — a hybrid model treats *"these legacy systems as authoritative sources for issuing W3C Verifiable Credentials"*【10†L95】.

**Trade-off: Pure SSI vs Hybrid:** Pure self-sovereign identity models face significant enterprise adoption barriers. ChainScore reports *"\~90% user drop-off"* for pure SSI due to the requirement for users to manage cryptographic keys, creating *"untenable legal liability on corporations for lost credentials"*【10†L36】. The hybrid approach mitigates this by providing a custodial wallet layer abstracting key management, while anchoring recovery mechanisms to decentralized networks — resulting in an estimated *"10x adoption lift"* and *"-70% support cost"* compared to pure SSI【10†L70-L73】. The European Self-Sovereign Identity Framework (ESSIF) mandates this exact hybrid pattern, *"using the EBSI ledger for anchoring while member states operate their own credential issuers"*【10†L23】.

***

## Risk Management & Trade-offs

| **Risk**                                  | **Impact**                                      | **Mitigation**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| ----------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Developer pushback**                    | Adoption delay, workarounds that defeat purpose | Automate via templates, learning mode, clear documentation. The Zero-Trust reference implementation demonstrates that secure tool extensions can be *"relatively small in code but high impact in design"* — adding governance does not require rewriting agent code【9†L75】. |
| **Missing capabilities causing failures** | Agent unable to complete tasks                  | "Fail closed with feedback" — when denied, the system logs exactly which capability was missing, accelerating ACM iteration. Temporary "wildcard capability" option in non-production with monitoring.                                                                       |
| **Coverage gaps (unprotected tools)**     | Residual ambient authority                      | Inventory all agent I/O paths. Code review and dynamic analysis. Phase 1 containment ensures broad protection before Phase 2 tightens.                                                                                                                                       |
| **LLM controlling security procedures**   | Security bypass                                 | Research confirms *"limitations once an agent's LLM is in sole charge to control the respective security procedures"*【1†L40】. Our design explicitly places all security controls outside the LLM's control — the model proposes, the runtime verifies.                       |
| **DID resolution latency**                | Cross-org call overhead                         | Use `did:web` for low-latency resolution within enterprise contexts (piggybacking on DNS/HTTPS). Cache DID Documents. ChainScore reports \~500ms verify time for hybrid VC verification【10†L56-L57】.                                                                         |
| **Key compromise**                        | Token forgery, impersonation                    | Short-lived tokens (15-minute TTL), Key Vault RBAC and audit logging, key rotation policies (new version every 90 days).                                                                                                                                                     |
| **Cross-org trust bootstrapping**         | Inability to verify partner credentials         | Pre-exchange DID Documents or participate in trust communities. Microsoft's approach: *"linked domains"* in DID Documents allow cross-referencing DNS and DID trust【2†L65】.                                                                                                  |

***

## Azure-First but Cloud-Portable Pilot Readiness Checklist

Before expanding to full production or additional integration points, ensure the following:

*   **✅ Azure Identity Integration:** All agent actions require valid, unexpired capability tokens issued by the enterprise (Azure AD) or a trusted partner. Agents cannot call Azure resources or external APIs without a traceable token. Azure AD Conditional Access policies are enforced at token issuance.

*   **✅ Enforcement via Azure APIM Gateway:** Every tool or API behind a gateway that validates tokens using the `validate-jwt` policy with the enterprise's signing keys【5†L269】. No agent can directly invoke back-end services. All privileged actions correlated with identity and checked against policy.

*   **✅ Least Privilege:** Capabilities scoped to minimum necessary actions. For Azure resources: least-privileged roles or SAS tokens. For database access: only permitted queries on specific tables. Default deny for unspecified actions.

*   **✅ Cryptographic Audit Trail:** Security-relevant events recorded in tamper-evident form via Azure Key Vault signed digests【5†L287-L289】. Logs stored securely with immutable retention policies. Audit records capture who (agent + principal), what (action/resource), when, and under which authorization (token ID).

*   **✅ Decentralized Identity Ready:** Each agent has a unique ID (DID or GUID) usable across system boundaries. Enterprise has a means to prove credential authenticity via DIDs. Trusted partners can validate agent credentials by resolving the enterprise DID and verifying signatures without Azure AD dependency at runtime. Conversely, system configured to trust specific external issuer DIDs for partner credentials.

*   **✅ Azure Monitoring & Alerts Operational:** Azure Monitor and Sentinel actively monitoring the pilot. Key metrics within expected ranges. Alerts tuned for security anomalies: repeated denied actions, unusual delegation chains, expired credential usage. Defender for Cloud CSPM enabled for AI agent posture management【5†L351】.

*   **✅ Performance Benchmarks Met:** Token validation overhead ≤1ms per call through APIM. Issuer adds <1 second to agent launch. System tested for memory/CPU leaks under load. Autoscaling configured for critical components.

*   **✅ Kill-Switch Operational:** Global and session-scoped kill mechanisms implemented outside the agent runtime. Tested in staging with successful results. Responsible personnel trained on usage.

*   **✅ Revocation & Expiry:** Tokens short-lived (15-minute default). System handles renewal for long sessions. Immediate revocation via distributed cache consulted by all Gateways. Expired and revoked tokens confirmed rejected.

*   **✅ Cloud Portability Validated:** Design choices favor open standards and cloud-neutral components:
    *   **Identity:** Using OIDC/SAML means IdP can be swapped. DID/VC formats follow W3C standards.
    *   **Distributed trust:** Trust established through verifiable credential exchange rather than upstream integration.
    *   **Multi-cloud infrastructure:** All core functions (containerized runtime, identity issuance, policy enforcement, logging) deployable on other clouds with documented mappings.
    *   The pilot confirms the **Azure-optimized implementation is not Azure-locked**. The hybrid model ensures enterprise systems issue and manage credentials (for compliance and control) while decentralized mechanisms provide cross-platform verification【10†L17-L25】.

*   **✅ Documentation & Training:** All documentation complete and disseminated. Capability configuration, error interpretation, incident response, and DID/VC verification procedures documented.

*   **✅ Security Sign-off:** Security review completed. Critical vulnerabilities resolved. System compliant with relevant security standards. Residual risks documented.

*   **✅ Rollback Plan:** In case of unforeseen problems, a rollback strategy is ready (disable capability enforcement and revert, or deactivate agent entirely).

***

With these milestones completed, the Capability-Native Governance system is ready for broader deployment. The architecture fuses Azure's identity and security services with open standards (OIDC, OAuth2, DIDs, VCs) to create a system where every autonomous agent carries verifiable credentials and is subject to continuous, deterministic policy enforcement. The approach treats each AI agent as a first-class identity that must authenticate and be authorized for each action — *"zero-trust for AI agents is now not just theory but practice"*【9†L45】. The Azure-first implementation ensures rapid pilot deployment, while adherence to W3C DID/VC standards and the pluggable identity architecture guarantees the design extends across multiple clouds and organizational boundaries without fundamental changes.
